elements.sent-2411-001: We present a two-dimensional delta symbol method that facilitates a version of the Kloosterman refinement of the circle method, addressing a question posed by Heath-Brown.
elements.sent-2411-002: As an application, we establish the asymptotic formula for the number of integral points on a non-singular intersection of two integral quadratic forms with at least $10$ variables.
elements.sent-2411-003: Assuming the Generalized Lindel\"of Hypothesis, we reduce the number of variables to $9$ by performing a double Kloosterman refinement.
elements.sent-2411-004: A heuristic argument suggests our two-dimensional delta symbol will typically outperform known expressions of this type by an increasing margin as the number of variables grows.
elements.section-2411-001: Overview of a delta symbol method
elements.sent-2411-005: Consider a system of $R$ homogeneous degree $d$ integral forms in $s$ variables.
elements.sent-2411-006: We aim to derive an asymptotic formula for the number of integral solutions of size at most $P$ to this system, as $P$ approaches infinity.
elements.sent-2411-007: A straightforward heuristic suggests that there are roughly $P^{s-dR}$ integral solutions as long as $s>dR$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Indeed, such asymptotic formulae can be obtained using the circle method if $s$ is sufficiently large in terms of $d$ and $R$.
elements.sent-2411-009: It is an interesting question whether one can reduce the required size of $s$ in these asymptotic formulae.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we focus on the case when $d=R=2$ and prove the following result for non-singular intersections of two quadrics.
elements.sent-2411-011: Let $F_1, F_2$ be two quadratic forms with integral coefficients in $s\geq 10$ variables.
elements.sent-2411-012: Suppose the projective variety defined by $F_1(\x)=F_2(\x)=0$ is non-singular of codimension 2.
elements.sent-2411-014: Then for any $\Delta< 1/6$, we have \begin{equation}\label{asymtwoquadrics} \operatornamewithlimits{\sum}_{\substack{\x\in \ZZ^s F_1(\x)=F_2(\x)=0}}w\Big(\frac{\x}{P}\Big)=\mathfrak S \mathfrak I P^{s-4}+O(P^{s-4-\Delta}),\end{equation} where the singular series $\mathfrak S$ defined in \eqref{eq:SS depends on $F_1, F_2$ and the singular integral $\mathfrak I$ defined in \eqref{eq:SI depends on $F_1, F_2$ and $w$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The implicit constant in the error term depends on \(\Delta,F_1,F_2\) and \(w\).
elements.sent-2411-016: Under the Generalized Lindel\"of Hypothesis (GLH) for Dirichlet $L$-functions, \eqref{asymtwoquadrics also holds for $s=9$ with $\Delta<1/15$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The asymptotic formula \eqref{asymtwoquadrics verifies the Manin--Peyre conjecture for nonsingular complete intersections of two quadrics with dimension at least $7$ (or $6$ under GLH), improving an earlier result of Munshi~\cite{M , which requires $s\geq 11$ with $\Delta <1/32$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Our condition $s\geq 10$ matches that in the work of Heath-Brown--Pierce~\cite{HBP , which handles the ``split" case when $F_i(\mathbf x,\mathbf y)=G_i(\mathbf x)+H_i(\mathbf y)$ where $G_i,H_i$ are quadratic forms in at least $5$ variables for $i=1,2$ with $\Delta<1/32$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The conditional part of our result $s\geq 9$ matches the analogous result in the function field setting obtained by Vishe \cite{V19 , where GLH follows from the generalized Riemann Hypothesis over finite fields.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: Variations of the circle method: a history
elements.sent-2411-029: The classical circle method from Hardy, Littlewood, Ramanujan and Vinogradov writes $\delta_n$ as $\int_{0 ^1 e(\alpha n)d\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: One then analyzes the quantity in question by breaking the unit circle into major and minor arcs.
elements.sent-2411-031: The major arc contributions are estimated asymptotically leading to the expected main terms, while the minor arc contributions are only estimated with an upper bound which should be in the error terms.
elements.sent-2411-032: This type of argument allows one to obtain the analytic Hasse Principle (for the exact meaning of this term see Arala \cite{Arala ), when $s$ is sufficiently large.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Building on the work of Davenport \cite{Davenport , Birch \cite{Birch established the analytic Hasse Principle for a smooth complete intersection defined by $R$ forms of degree $d$ in $s$ variables over $\QQ$ when $s>(d-1)2^{d-1 R(R+1)+R-1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Improvements of Birch's result have been made in many cases.
elements.sent-2411-035: In particular, the quadratic dependency on $R$ has been reduced to linear in the work of Rydin Myerson \cite{Myerson by estimating the minor arc contributions using repulsions in exponential sums.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Another type of refinement of the circle method, aimed at reducing the number of required variables, avoids the traditional splitting of the unit circle into major and minor arcs and instead treats the contributions of all arcs asymptotically.
elements.sent-2411-037: This imitates the method of Kloosterman \cite{Kloosterman who used the Farey dissection of the unit circle to obtain the analytic Hasse Principle for representations of integers by positive definite quaternary quadratic forms.}
elements.sent-2411-038: This bypasses the barrier encountered in the Hardy--Littlewood type circle method which requires at least five variables (see also Birch \cite{Birch for $R=1, d=2$).}
elements.sent-2411-039: The advantage of the Kloosterman refinement is to make use of cancellations between complete exponential sums when averaged over different rationals with the same denominator.
elements.sent-2411-040: One can obtain a version of the Kloosterman refinement without appealing to the Farey dissection by using the delta symbol method, originated from the work of Duke-Friedlander-Iwaniec \cite{DFI and further developed by Heath-Brown \cite{Heath-Brown96 .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The delta symbol method essentially provides a smooth partition of the unit circle (see Marmon-Vishe \cite[Proposition 1.2]{Marmon_Vishe ) of the form \begin{equation}\delta_{n} = \sum_{1\leq q\leq Q} \underset{\hphantom{a\bmod{q}}}{\starsum_{a\bmod{q}}} \int_{-Q^\delta/qQ}^{Q^\delta/qQ} p_{q}(w) e((a/q+w)n)\,dw +O_{\delta,N}(Q^{-N}),\end{equation} for all \(n\in\ZZ\) and all \(\delta,N,Q>0\).}
elements.sent-2411-042: This not only allows one to carry out the Kloosterman refinement more easily, by switching the sum and the integral to make use of averages over the $a$-sum, but also allows one to take advantage of the average over the \(q\)-sum.
elements.sent-2411-043: That is, one can use cancellations in averages over rationals with different denominators, performing a double Kloosterman refinement, a key technique in Heath-Brown \cite{Heath-Brown96 for studying integral solutions to a single quadratic form in at least $3$ variables.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The delta symbol method has seen many other applications in recent years, such as shifted convolution problems and subconvexity estimates for $L$-functions (see e.g. \cite{Munshi0, Munshi1, Munshi2, Munshi3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-045: There are also delta symbol methods over number fields by Browning--Vishe~\cite{BV and over central simple division algebras over number fields (for example, quaternions over $\QQ$) by Arala--Getz--Hou--Hsu--Li--Wang~\cite{AGHHLW .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: It is natural to ask whether an analogue of the Kloosterman refinement of the circle method can be obtained in higher dimensions, a problem posed by Heath-Brown \cite{Heath-Brown96 for dimension two.}
elements.sent-2411-047: There have been various attempts of a two-dimensional Kloosterman refinement over $\QQ$, including work of Browning--Munshi \cite{BM , Munshi \cite{M , Heath-Brown--Pierce \cite{HBP and Arala~\cite{Arala for pairs of quadratic forms and Northey--Vishe \cite{NV for pairs of cubic forms.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The work of Pierce--Schindler--Wood \cite{PSW and Browning--Pierce--Schindler \cite{BPS even extends such a process to higher dimensions.}}

elements.sent-2411-556: Combining \eqref{eq:1001 and \eqref{eq:1011 , we obtain \begin{equation}N_{2,2}(P, \delta) \ll P^{\ve} \max_{\mathcal Q_\delta}Y^{-s/2+3/2}D^{3/2}KWQ^{-3/2} P^{s}(1+P^2W)^{-s/2} V^{s-1}C\min\{V(1+P^2W)^{1/2},C\} \quad +\max_{\mathcal Q_\delta}Y^{-s/2+3/2}D^{3/2}KWQ^{-3/2} P^{s}(1+P^2W)^{-s/2} V^{s-2}C((1+P^2W)^{1/2}+C) \ll P^{\ve} \max_{\mathcal Q_\delta} Y^{-s/2+3/2}D^{1/2}WQ^{-1} P^{s}(1+P^2W)^{-s/2}C^{1/2}(1+P^2W)^{1/4}V^{s-1/2} \quad+ \max_{\mathcal Q_\delta}Y^{-s/2+3/2}D^{1/2}WQ^{-1} P^{s}(1+P^2W)^{-s/2}((1+P^2W)^{1/2}+C)V^{s-2} \ll P^{\ve}\max_{\mathcal Q_\delta}Y^{-s/2+3/2}D^{1/2}WQ^{-1} P^{s}(1+P^2W)^{-s/2} \qquad\times (C^{1/2}(1+P^2W)^{1/4}+((1+P^2W)^{1/2}+C)V^{-3/2})V^{s-1/2}.\label{eq:102}\end{equation} Comparing the powers of $D,C$ and $K$ in the above expression, since $KDC\asymp Q^{1/2}$, the maximum of the above expression is attained when $C\asymp Q^{1/2 ,DK\asymp 1$ and this the above is \begin{equation}\ll P^{\ve} \max_{\mathcal Q_\delta}Y^{-s/2+3/2}WQ^{-1} P^{s}(1+P^2W)^{-s/2}(Q^{1/4}(1+P^2W)^{1/4}+((1+P^2W)^{1/2}+Q^{1/2})V^{-3/2})V^{s-1/2}.\label{eq:1021}\end{equation} Recall $V=YP^{-1 (1+P^2W)P^\ve$ and $W\ll Y^{-1 Q^{-1/2 P^\delta$, we see that \eqref{eq:102 again takes maximum when $W\asymp Y^{-1 Q^{-1/2 P^\delta$, which means $1+P^2W\asymp (Q/Y)P^\delta$ and $V\asymp P^\ve P^\delta Q/P=P^{1/3+\ve+\delta}$.}}}}}}}}}
